MATH 341 — ODE Method Selector

University of Scranton  ·  Fall 2026  ·  A guide to classifying ODEs and choosing a solution strategy

Classification question Solution method Key result / formula Special sub-case Nonlinear / qualitative
Step 1 — What is the order and structure?
Start here
How many independent variables does the unknown function have?
Write the equation in standard form and look at the derivatives present.
One variable (function of t only)
ODE — ordinary differential equation
What is the order?
The order equals the highest derivative present.
1st order
Classification
Is it linear?
Can it be written as \(x' + p(t)\,x = q(t)\)?
If yes → linear. If \(x\) appears in a nonlinear way (e.g. \(x^2\), \(\sin x\)) → nonlinear.
Linear
Sub-case
Is it also separable?
Can you write it as \(\frac{dx}{g(x)} = f(t)\,dt\)?
Yes
Special case
Both methods work
Use whichever is easier. Separation of variables is often faster here.
No
Method
Integrating factor
Write in standard form \(x' + p(t)\,x = q(t)\).
Compute \(\mu(t) = e^{\int p(t)\,dt}\).
Multiply through: \(\bigl(\mu x\bigr)' = \mu q\). Integrate both sides.
Nonlinear
Sub-case
Is it separable?
Can you factor as \(x' = g(t)\cdot f(x)\)?
Yes
Method
Separation of variables
Separate: \(\displaystyle\int\frac{dx}{f(x)} = \int g(t)\,dt + C\).
Solve implicitly or explicitly for \(x\). Check for singular solutions where \(f(x)=0\).
No
Qualitative / Numerical
Qualitative or numerical approach
Draw a slope field. If autonomous (\(x'=f(x)\)), use phase-line analysis (§Notes 3). Use Euler or RK4 for a numerical solution (§Notes 14).
2nd order, linear, constant-coeff.
Classification
Is it homogeneous?
Write as \(x'' + px' + qx = f(t)\). Homogeneous means \(f(t)\equiv 0\).
\(f(t)\equiv 0\)
Method
Characteristic equation
Substitute \(x=e^{\lambda t}\) → \(\lambda^2+p\lambda+q=0\).
Discriminant \(\Delta = p^2-4q\):
  • \(\Delta>0\): \(x=c_1e^{\lambda_1 t}+c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\)
  • \(\Delta=0\): \(x=(c_1+c_2 t)e^{\lambda t}\)
  • \(\Delta<0\): \(x=e^{\alpha t}(c_1\cos\beta t+c_2\sin\beta t)\)
\(f(t)\not\equiv 0\)
Strategy
Find \(x_h\) first, then \(x_p\)
General solution: \(x = x_h + x_p\). Which method for \(x_p\)?
\(f(t)\) is polynomial, exp, sin/cos
Method
Undetermined coefficients
Guess \(x_p\) in the same family as \(f(t)\). Substitute and match coefficients.
Modification rule: if the guess duplicates a term in \(x_h\), multiply the guess by \(t\).
Watch for resonance when \(f(t)=F_0\cos(\omega t)\) and \(\omega\) is the natural frequency.
Any \(f(t)\) (general)
Method
Variation of parameters
With \(x_1,x_2\) a fundamental set and Wronskian \(W=x_1x_2'-x_1'x_2\): \[x_p = -x_1\!\int\!\frac{x_2 f}{W}\,dt + x_2\!\int\!\frac{x_1 f}{W}\,dt\] Works for any \(f(t)\), including non-constant coefficients (Notes 5).
Several variables
Out of scope
Partial differential equation (PDE)
This course focuses exclusively on ODEs. PDEs (heat equation, wave equation, Laplace equation) are covered in MATH 441.
Step 2 — Laplace transform method (alternative for IVPs)
When to use Laplace transforms
Is the ODE a linear IVP with constant coefficients and a piecewise or impulsive forcing?
Laplace transforms are especially powerful when \(f(t)\) involves a Heaviside step function \(u(t-a)\) or a Dirac delta \(\delta(t-a)\) — cases where undetermined coefficients and variation of parameters are awkward. They also handle standard forcing (\(e^{at}\), \(\sin, \cos\), polynomials) and work for any-order constant-coefficient linear IVP.
Method — Notes 6, 7, 8
Four-step Laplace procedure
  1. Transform. Apply \(\mathcal{L}\) to both sides. Use \(\mathcal{L}\{x'\} = sX - x(0)\) and \(\mathcal{L}\{x''\} = s^2X - sx(0) - x'(0)\) to incorporate initial conditions immediately.
  2. Solve algebraically. The transformed equation is algebraic in \(X(s)\). Solve for \(X(s)\).
  3. Simplify. Use partial fractions, the s-shift theorem \(\mathcal{L}\{e^{at}f\}=F(s-a)\), the t-shift theorem \(\mathcal{L}\{u(t-a)f(t-a)\}=e^{-as}F(s)\), and/or the convolution theorem \(\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F\cdot G\}=f*g\).
  4. Invert. Read \(x(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{X(s)\}\) from the table.

Key table entries: \(\mathcal{L}\{e^{at}\}=\tfrac{1}{s-a}\), \(\;\mathcal{L}\{t^n\}=\tfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}\), \(\;\mathcal{L}\{\sin bt\}=\tfrac{b}{s^2+b^2}\), \(\;\mathcal{L}\{\cos bt\}=\tfrac{s}{s^2+b^2}\), \(\;\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t-a)\}=e^{-as}\).

Step 3 — First-order linear systems \(\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}\)
Notes 9 – 11
Is the system \(\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}(t)\) homogeneous?
General solution always has the form \(\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_h + \mathbf{x}_p\). Find \(\mathbf{x}_h\) first via eigenvalues.
Method — homogeneous part
Eigenvalue method for \(\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}\)
Solve \(\det(A-\lambda I)=0\) for eigenvalues \(\lambda\), then \((A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}\) for eigenvectors \(\mathbf{v}\).
Distinct real \(\lambda_1\neq\lambda_2\)
General solution
Two straight-line solutions
\[\mathbf{x}=c_1e^{\lambda_1 t}\mathbf{v}_1+c_2e^{\lambda_2 t}\mathbf{v}_2\] Phase portrait: node or saddle depending on signs of \(\lambda_1,\lambda_2\).
Complex \(\lambda=\alpha\pm\beta i\)
General solution
Real & imaginary parts
Use \(\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{p}+i\mathbf{q}\): \[c_1 e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf{p}\cos\beta t-\mathbf{q}\sin\beta t)+c_2 e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf{p}\sin\beta t+\mathbf{q}\cos\beta t)\] Phase portrait: spiral (\(\alpha\neq 0\)) or center (\(\alpha=0\)).
Repeated real \(\lambda_1=\lambda_2\)
Special case
May need generalized eigenvector
If \(A\) has two independent eigenvectors: proceed as distinct case. Otherwise find generalized eigenvector \(\mathbf{w}\): \((A-\lambda I)\mathbf{w}=\mathbf{v}\). See Notes 10–11.
If \(\mathbf{b}(t)\neq\mathbf{0}\) — nonhomogeneous system
Find a particular solution \(\mathbf{x}_p\)
Use undetermined coefficients (if \(\mathbf{b}(t)\) is polynomial/exponential/sinusoidal) or variation of parameters. General solution: \(\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{x}_h+\mathbf{x}_p\). See Notes 11.
Step 4 — Nonlinear systems and phase plane analysis
Notes 12 – 13
Is the system nonlinear: \(x'=f(x,y),\; y'=g(x,y)\)?
No closed-form solution in general. Use qualitative and numerical methods.
Strategy — qualitative analysis
Phase plane procedure
  1. Find equilibria. Solve \(f(x,y)=0\) and \(g(x,y)=0\) simultaneously.
  2. Nullclines. Sketch \(x\)-nullclines (\(f=0\), motion purely vertical) and \(y\)-nullclines (\(g=0\), motion purely horizontal). Intersections are equilibria.
  3. Linearize. Compute the Jacobian \(J=\bigl(\begin{smallmatrix}f_x&f_y\\g_x&g_y\end{smallmatrix}\bigr)\) at each equilibrium and classify via eigenvalues of \(J\) (see Step 3 and the table below).
  4. Sketch trajectories. Use nullcline signs and equilibrium types to draw the global phase portrait. Confirm with a numerical solver when needed.
Step 5 — Numerical methods (when exact methods fail or are impractical)
Notes 14
Do you need approximate numerical values of a solution?
Use when: the ODE is nonlinear with no closed form, or the exact solution is too complex to be practical.
Quick estimate, large \(h\) OK
1st-order method
Euler's method
\(t_{n+1}=t_n+h,\quad y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(t_n,y_n)\).
Global error \(O(h)\). Simple but accumulates error quickly.
High accuracy needed
4th-order method
Runge–Kutta 4 (RK4)
Four slope evaluations \(k_1,k_2,k_3,k_4\) per step: \[y_{n+1}=y_n+\tfrac{h}{6}(k_1+2k_2+2k_3+k_4)\] Global error \(O(h^4)\). Standard choice in practice.
Quick-reference summary
ODE type Key test Method Course notes
1st-order, linear Can be written \(x'+p(t)x=q(t)\) Integrating factor \(\mu=e^{\int p\,dt}\) Notes 2, §1.4
1st-order, separable Right side factors as \(g(t)\cdot f(x)\) Separation of variables Notes 2, §1.3
1st-order, autonomous \(x'=f(x)\), no explicit \(t\) Phase line, equilibria & stability Notes 3, §1.5
2nd-order, linear, homogeneous, const. coeff. \(x''+px'+qx=0\) Characteristic equation (3 cases via \(\Delta\)) Notes 4, §2.2
2nd-order, nonhomogeneous, const. coeff. — "nice" \(f\) \(f(t)\) is polynomial, \(e^{at}\), \(\sin/\cos\), or products Undetermined coefficients (+ modification rule) Notes 4, §2.3
2nd-order, nonhomogeneous — general \(f\) Any \(f(t)\); variable or constant coefficients Variation of parameters Notes 5, §2.3–2.4
Any linear const. coeff. IVP, esp. with \(u(t)\) or \(\delta(t)\) Initial values given; piecewise or impulsive forcing Laplace transform (4-step procedure) Notes 6–8, Ch. 3
Linear system \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\) System of 1st-order linear ODEs in matrix form Eigenvalue–eigenvector method Notes 9–11, Ch. 4
Nonlinear system \(x'=f(x,y),\,y'=g(x,y)\) Cannot be linearized globally Nullclines, linearization via Jacobian, phase portrait Notes 12–13, Ch. 5
Any IVP (numerical) Closed-form solution unavailable or impractical Euler's method (1st order) or RK4 (4th order) Notes 14, Ch. 6

MATH 341 Differential Equations  ·  University of Scranton  ·  Fall 2026  ·  Textbook: Logan, A First Course in Differential Equations, 3rd ed.  ·  Course site: math341diffeq.netlify.app
